subject: Ecology
title: Alligators as Keystone Species
description: A short lesson to help you apply Alligators as Keystone Species.

hook.text: Alligators are not just predators. They're landscape architects that reshape wetlands.

buildup.text: They dig 'gator holes'—depressions that hold water during dry periods when marshes shrink.

discovery.text: Dozens of species—fish, turtles, snails, birds—crowd into gator holes to survive drought.

twist.text: Without alligators, many Everglades species would lose their only dry-season water refuge.

climax.text: Alligators are keystone species: remove them and the entire wetland community restructures.

punchline.text: The most dangerous animal in the marsh keeps it alive.

quiz.question: What are gator holes?
options: Depressions dug by alligators that hold water in dry periods
